title: Resolving Pronoun References
concept: In 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was tired', self-attention links 'it' strongly to 'animal' based on adjective associations.

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of self-attention in a language model?

options.text: To find connections between words in a sentence
To translate words into different languages instantly

exercises.explain: Self-attention calculates how words relate to each other in a sentence to understand its overall meaning.

cards.title: Who is "It"?
cards.body: When you read the word "it", your brain instantly knows what it refers to. But how does a computer do this? It doesn't have common sense—instead, it uses a smart math trick called self-attention to connect the dots!

cards.title: What is Pronoun Resolution?
cards.body: Pronoun resolution is the process of figuring out exactly which noun a pronoun (like "he", "she", or "it") refers to. Self-attention makes this happen by calculating connection strengths between the pronoun and all other words in the sentence, paying close attention to descriptive words.

cards.title: The Party Guest Analogy
cards.body: Imagine a party where someone says, "Look at him go!" If you want to know who "him" is, you look around the room at what everyone is doing. Self-attention does this in text: it looks at all the surrounding words to find which person or object a pronoun like "it" or "him" is actually pointing to.

cards.title: A Tale of Two Sentences
cards.body:
Take these two sentences:
1. "The animal didn't cross the street because it was tired." -> "it" links to "animal" because "tired" fits living things.
2. "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too wide." -> "it" links to "street" because "wide" describes roads.
Self-attention instantly shifts its focus based on these adjectives!

cards.title: Not Just Grammar Rules!
cards.body: You might think AI resolves pronouns by using rigid grammar rules. Actually, it uses self-attention to weigh the probabilities of words based on context clues, like connecting "tired" to "animal" because of how they often appear together.

cards.title: The Attention Shift
cards.body: See how self-attention dynamically shifts its strongest connection based on the adjective at the end of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Why does self-attention link 'it' to 'animal' in: 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was tired'?

options.text: The adjective 'tired' is strongly associated with 'animal' rather than 'street'
The word 'animal' comes first in the sentence
The word 'street' is longer than the word 'animal'
The model randomly guesses which noun to choose

exercises.explain: The adjective 'tired' is highly associated with living creatures rather than paved roads. Self-attention uses this context clue to connect 'it' to 'animal'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Changing a single word at the end of a sentence can completely change which noun self-attention links a pronoun to.
exercises.explain: Self-attention changes its connections based on context. If the adjective changes (e.g., from 'tired' to 'wide'), the connection for 'it' shifts from 'animal' to 'street'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of how AI connects pronouns.
exercises.sentence: AI models use self-___ to link pronouns to their correct nouns.
exercises.explain: Self-attention links pronouns like 'it' to the correct noun by examining all the words in the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Identify the correct behavior of a self-attention model processing 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was tired':

options.text: AI links 'it' to 'animal' because of the adjective 'tired'
AI links 'it' to 'street' because streets get tired
AI leaves 'it' unconnected because it cannot decide

exercises.explain: Option A shows the correct connection because 'tired' is a state of a living thing. Option B is incorrect because streets do not get tired. Option C is incorrect because self-attention does not connect pronouns randomly.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence "The animal didn't cross the street because it was too narrow", how does self-attention resolve the pronoun "it"?

options.text: The animal, because animals are usually narrow
The street, because 'narrow' describes a road's physical space
Both equally, splitting the connection in half

exercises.explain: The word 'it' points to the street because the word 'narrow' describes physical dimensions typical of a pathway, not a biological state like fatigue. Self-attention weights these context clues to resolve the reference.
